notes: State enabling authority is verified. The atlas maps San Francisco San Diego 38 current City of Los Angeles BIDs eight operational assessment districts in Santa Monica from the official GIS and adopted PBAD maps ten Oakland districts eleven active San Jose districts eight of Long Beach's nine current BIDs all three current West Hollywood districts 23 Sacramento PBIDs BIDs and BIAs all six current Berkeley districts all four current Pasadena districts and the current tourism improvement districts in Anaheim and Garden Grove and Costa Mesa's current Tourism and Promotion Business Improvement Area. Berkeley's Elmwood Solano Telegraph and Tourism districts use exact polygon services; the Downtown and North Shattuck boundaries are generalized from official adopted plans and street limits. Pasadena's tourism district uses the exact official city boundary; Old Pasadena Playhouse Village and South Lake Avenue are generalized from adopted management plans and official street limits. Anaheim's boundary is vector-derived from the city's adopted January 2026 district map and georeferenced against the official Anaheim Resort GIS boundary; it reflects the December 2025 expansion and covers The Anaheim Resort and Platinum Triangle. Garden Grove's Harbor Boulevard hotel corridor boundary is generalized from the official unchanged boundary description because the precise Ordinance No. 2782 map is held by the City Clerk but is not published as GIS; the FY2026-27 report confirms Tier I north of Lampson Avenue and Tier II south of Lampson Avenue. Costa Mesa's FY2026-27 renewal and founding ordinance define its benefit zone as the entire city. Dana Point's May 2025 renewal continues its citywide lodging assessment through June 2030; the atlas uses the official County of Orange city boundary for that benefit zone. Huntington Beach's FY2025-26 records confirm both its 2004 Downtown BID and its 2014 Tourism BID; the downtown boundary uses the city's exact nine-part BID Zones layer and the tourism benefit zone uses the official city boundary because it covers lodging businesses and short-term rentals citywide. Laguna Beach renewed its citywide Tourism Marketing District in June 2025 through June 2035; the atlas uses the official County of Orange city boundary for all assessed lodging businesses. Manhattan Beach's FY2026 budget and current governance records confirm both the Downtown and North Manhattan Beach BIDs; their atlas polygons are generalized from the official street-boundary descriptions. Burlingame's May 2026 assessment approvals and current municipal code confirm its Broadway and Burlingame Avenue merchant BIDs; their atlas polygons are generalized from the official commercial-area exhibits. San Mateo's active Downtown Business Improvement Area was established in 1986 and retains the two-zone parcel boundary expanded in 2008; its atlas polygon is a generalized envelope of the official adopted parcel map. Redwood City's Downtown Community Benefit Improvement District covers 222 assessed parcels and runs through December 2029; its atlas polygon is generalized from the adopted Brewster Veterans Maple and Pennsylvania/El Camino Real limits. South San Francisco's October 2025 record still describes its Downtown PBID as in formation so it is not yet mapped. Santa Cruz established its Midtown merchant BID for calendar 2026; its generalized atlas boundary follows the adopted Soquel Water Seabright and Morrissey corridor description. Santa Cruz renewed both established downtown districts for FY2027: the merchant Parking and Business Improvement Area created in 1990 and the property-based Cooperative Retail Management district created in 1994. Their atlas polygons are generalized from the municipal-code street limits and the official parcel map for the CRM boundary expanded in 2023. Santa Monica’s Downtown PBAD was established in 2008 and affirmed through 2028; the Colorado Avenue Overlay and Lincoln Boulevard PBAD were established in 2015 through the same end date. Their generalized atlas polygons follow the official benefit-zone map. The separately proposed South Lincoln Boulevard PBAD did not advance beyond the documented 2019 draft formation process and is excluded Downtown Fresno’s PBID was organized in 2010 and renewed for 2023 through 2032; its atlas polygon is a generalized envelope of the 352 assessed parcels shown on the official renewal map. Downtown Napa’s PBID was established in 2005 and renewed in June 2025 through December 2032. The atlas uses exact current city parcel geometry for 272 of the 273 assessed parcels in the adopted plan; plan-era APN 044-250-030-000 is absent from the live parcel layer. Downtown Merced’s PBID was formed in 2023 for an initial operating term from January 2024 through December 2028. Its generalized atlas boundary follows the adopted parcel and benefit-zone map and the official 16th R 18th/19th and G Street limits. Palm Springs established its citywide Small Hotel Tourism Business Improvement District in 2016 for lodging businesses with 49 rooms or fewer and initiated its FY2026-27 levy in June 2026. The atlas uses the exact official city boundary for the benefit zone. Riverside maintains three active districts: the Downtown Parking and Business Improvement Area established in 1985, Arlington BID established in 2002 and expanded in 2025, and Auto Center BID established in 2011. Their generalized atlas polygons follow the official Downtown and Arlington maps and the current municipal-code limits for Auto Center. A July 2026 audit found no active Bakersfield BID or PBID. The Downtown Business Association operates through voluntary membership, the adopted station-area vision identifies a downtown BID only as a possible future tool, and the City FY2026-27 proposed budget contains no business-improvement assessment fund. Stockton has three active assessment districts: the Downtown Stockton PBID established in 1997, the Miracle Mile CID formed in 2023 through 2042, and the citywide Tourism BID established in 2010 and renewed through 2035. Downtown is mapped under its current PBID identity through 2026; its approved successor CID begins January 1, 2027. Downtown and Miracle Mile use generalized official map boundaries, while the tourism benefit zone uses exact City open-data limits. A July 2026 audit found no active San Bernardino BID or PBID. The City lists 68 active Proposition 218 infrastructure and maintenance assessment districts, but its FY2026-27 budget contains no business-improvement or PBID fund. The downtown PBID remains a formation proposal under stakeholder review and is therefore not mapped. Ontario has two active assessment districts: the Downtown Ontario Community Benefit District established in 2019 and renewed in 2024 for ten years, and the Greater Ontario Tourism Marketing District established in 2013 with a current term through 2028. Downtown uses a generalized official parcel-map boundary; the tourism district uses exact official Ontario and Rancho Cucamonga city boundaries for assessed lodging businesses and short-term rentals. Apple Valley’s Village Property and Business Improvement District was established in 2007 and renewed in 2022 for a ten-year term. Its generalized atlas boundary follows the official Navajo-to-Tenaya/Central and Esaws-to-Ottawa limits around Highway 18. Glendora’s Village Business Improvement District was established in 2009 and renewed its assessment for FY2026-27 with no boundary changes. Its generalized atlas polygon follows the official map and municipal-code description from Bennett Avenue to Foothill Boulevard between Vermont and Vista Bonita, including the prescribed southwest extension. Downtown Pomona’s PBID was formed in 2004 and renewed through 2028. Its generalized atlas multipolygon follows the official 2019 parcel map retained with the FY2025-26 assessment levy. Culver City levied two merchant BIDs for calendar 2026: Downtown, established in 1998, and the Arts District, established in 2016. Both atlas polygons are generalized from the current official boundary exhibits and address or street limits. Monrovia’s Old Town merchant BID was formed in 1968 and remains active through the current City business-license assessment and FY2025-27 BID fund. Its generalized atlas boundary follows the official Foothill, Olive, Primrose, and Ivy street limits. A July 2026 audit found no evidence of a currently levied Huntington Park BID. The 1995 establishing ordinances and 2005-08 strategic plan remain online, but the FY2026-27 adopted budget contains no BID assessment fund or revenue; current Pacific Boulevard work uses CDBG funding and a new La Pacific Partners outreach effort. The legacy district is therefore not mapped pending a current levy record. A July 2026 audit found no evidence that Bellflower completed formation of either proposed Bellflower Boulevard PBID. The City’s October 2019 FAQ called them the first proposed districts in City history, its FY2020-21 ACFR still described one or two potential PBIDs, and the adopted FY2025-27 budget contains no PBID or business-improvement assessment fund or program. Bellflower is therefore not mapped pending a formation resolution and current levy. Whittier has two active Uptown assessment districts with exact City GIS boundaries: the property-based Uptown Community Benefit District formed in July 2017 and the older merchant Uptown Whittier Business Improvement Area. The City initiated its calendar-2026 merchant assessment levy in November 2025 and currently collects the fees through business licensing. The merchant area’s current benefit zones and assessment method date to Ordinance No. 2604 in 1993; the underlying area predates that modification. Burbank has two active districts: the Downtown PBID, formed in 2003 and renewed for 2019-2028, and the Tourism BID, formed in 2011 and renewed in November 2025 for July 2026 through June 2036. The tourism district uses the exact official California municipal boundary because it assesses Burbank lodging businesses with 25 or more rooms citywide; Downtown is generalized from the official parcel map. Magnolia Park's 2006 PBID sunset in 2011, and the City's current work is exploratory, so it is excluded. Pico Rivera does not yet have an operating BID: its current city page describes a Whittier Boulevard establishment effort begun with a 2021 funding commitment, the adopted April 2024 multimodal plan schedules BID development as a Phase 3 project five or more years out, and the FY2023-24 accomplishments report lists the district as an initiative. No assessment district is mapped until formation is completed. Glendale has two active assessment districts: the Greater Downtown Glendale Community Benefit District established in 2018 for a 20-year term and the longstanding Montrose Business Improvement District, for which the City issued a 2025 assessment return. Both atlas boundaries are generalized from official legal descriptions and adopted parcel maps because no public district GIS was located. Adams Square, established in 2004, was disestablished in 2019 and is excluded. South Gate's Tweedy Mile Parking and Business Improvement Area remains active through the current business-license assessment process. It was established in 1970; the atlas generalizes its footprint from the official parcel map and the City's complete street and address ranges. Compton does not have a verified operating BID. Municipal Code Chapter 9-3 provides a procedure for establishing a Business Promotion District but does not establish a named district; the current Compton Station TOD Specific Plan presents a Downtown PBID as a future implementation and financing strategy. No formation resolution, levy, or operating district registry was located, so no Compton boundary is mapped. Lynwood's citywide Parking and Business Improvement District was established by Ordinance 1262 in 1985; the FY2025-27 spending plan continues its 50% business-license assessment. The atlas uses the exact official city boundary. Paramount does not yet have an operating BID. The FY2024-25 budget identifies creation of a BID as a priority, the FY2025-26 budget allocates $75,000 to BID-related economic-development work, and the adopted North Paramount Gateway Specific Plan calls for future engagement to determine business interest. No formation resolution, assessment levy, or operating district registry was located, so no boundary is mapped. Lakewood, California has no verified operating BID in its current business portal or FY2024-26 adopted budget. Search results for the Alameda Corridor and West Colfax BIDs concern Lakewood, Colorado and are explicitly excluded from the California audit. A July 2026 audit found no verified operating BID in Bell Gardens. The current economic-development page lists the City’s business services without a district program, the FY2025-26 adopted-budget archive provides no formation or levy evidence, and the FY2024-25 ACFR lists 27 governmental funds without a business-improvement or special-assessment district. Search hits labeled “BID” on the procurement page are construction bid solicitations, not improvement districts, and are excluded.

Five active districts are now mapped: Honolulu SIDs Nos. 1-4 and Kailua Village BID No. 1.
notes: Statewide enabling is HRS §46-80.5 (Special improvement district), allowing charter counties to create SIDs by ordinance for supplemental services and improvements funded by special assessments. These are the legal BID equivalents. Honolulu currently implements four districts under ROH Ch. 28: No. 1 Waikīkī Business Improvement District (2000), No. 2 Fort Street Mall expanded and renamed Downtown Honolulu Business Improvement District by Ord. 25-47 (2025), No. 3 Waikīkī Beach Special Improvement District (2015), and No. 4 Waikīkī Transportation Management Special Improvement District (2017). Hawaiʻi County Code Ch. 35 has active Kailua Village BID No. 1; proposed Downtown Hilo BID No. 2 was rejected in 2023. Maui has enabling authority under Ch. 3.70 but no active district has yet been confirmed; Kauai planning material says the county had not authorized one. The atlas now maps all five verified active districts. Downtown Honolulu and Kailua Village boundaries were reconstructed from ordinance assessment parcel lists using current State parcel geometry; the three overlapping Waikīkī boundaries are generalized from official ordinance maps. No statewide registry or unified official GIS exists, so coverage remains substantial rather than comprehensive until negative findings for Maui and Kauai receive direct county confirmation.

notes: Illinois authorizes classic BIDs under Business Improvement District Law (65 ILCS 125/, P.A. 103-646, eff. 7-1-24), limited to municipalities >500,000 population (Chicago only). Chicago DPD accepts feasibility applications for BIDs (deadline Nov 1, 2026 for 2027 start); none active as of research (law recent; BIDs cannot overlap SSAs). Longstanding statewide equivalent is Special Service Areas under Special Service Area Tax Law (35 ILCS 200/27-5 et seq. / Article 27 of Property Tax Code), available to municipalities and counties for special services/improvements (including downtown/business corridor marketing, security, cleaning, beautification) funded by property tax levies solely on the SSA. Creation via local ordinance after notice, hearing, petition/objection process; maps/ordinances filed with county clerks/recorders. Chicago has ~58 active SSAs with authoritative provider lists, citywide maps, and downloadable GIS boundaries. Examples confirmed elsewhere (Peoria, Lake/Cook/Peoria/Kendall Counties GIS, Moline, Highland Park business SSAs, Aurora, Oak Park, Evanston, Yorkville, etc.). No central public statewide registry or inventory of all SSAs/BIDs located (IDOR has historically tracked 600+ for tax extension/property tax purposes as of ~2011, generating tens of millions annually, but no public list/GIS). Distinguish from unrelated 'Business Districts' under Illinois Municipal Code 65 ILCS 5/11-74.3 (sales-tax increment redevelopment of blighted areas). Coverage authoritative/complete for Chicago; partial for rest of state via scattered county GIS and municipal pages. Failed searches do not prove absence. Enabling URLs: BID https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/ILCS/Articles?ActID=4518&ChapterID=14 ; SSA article/sections via https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/ILCS/details?MajorTopic=&Chapter=&ActName=Property%20Tax%20Code.&ActID=596&ChapterID=8&ChapAct=35+ILCS+200%2F&SeqStart=86400000&SeqEnd=89400000 and https://ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/003502000K27-5.htm .

notes: Enabling statute is M.G.L. c. 40O (Business Improvement Districts), enacted ~1994; BIDs are contiguous areas ≥75% commercial/industrial/retail/mixed-use, formed by petition of ≥60% of property owners representing ≥51% assessed valuation, local hearing/approval, and filing with EOED. Official Mass.gov page and manual confirm process and that petitions are forwarded to EOED for technical assistance/compliance, but no public statewide registry, inventory, or GIS layer of all BIDs was located (failed searches are not proof of absence). Local term is consistently 'Business Improvement District' or 'BID'; distinguish carefully from Cultural Districts (Mass Cultural Council), Parking Benefit Districts, Tourism Destination Marketing Districts (TDMDs), District Improvement Financing (DIF), historic districts, and voluntary Main Street-style organizations (toolkit explicitly separates these). Known/operating examples (from official toolkit, case studies, district sites, and secondary confirmations such as ~10-11 total cited in Hyannis/New Bedford sources) include: Downtown Boston (Alliance), Greenway BID (Boston), Newmarket (Boston), Amherst, Central Square (Cambridge), Hyannis Main Street, Hudson, Springfield, Worcester, Taunton; Edgartown has public ArcGIS BID boundary (status may be active/proposed). No statewide map/GeoJSON found; local GIS/boundaries available for several. Secondary analyses (e.g. MassINC) use MassGIS parcels to examine select BIDs. Coverage remains partial pending authoritative full list or exhaustive municipal sweep.

local_terms: Parking and Business Improvement Area; PBIA; Business Improvement Area; BIA; Business Improvement District; BID; Tourism Improvement Area; Tourism Business Improvement Area
known_local_sources: Seattle Business Improvement Areas (list, ordinances, policies) [Seattle] https://www.seattle.gov/office-of-economic-development/business-districts/business-improvement-areas-; OED Business Improvement Area (BIA) Boundaries [Seattle] https://hub.arcgis.com/datasets/08107965cf3840668023f710e30796c3_0; Business Improvement Area (Tacoma) [Tacoma] https://data.tacoma.gov/datasets/tacoma::business-improvement-area-tacoma; Downtown Parking and Business Improvement Area / BID Board [Spokane] https://my.spokanecity.org/bcc/boards/downtown-business-improvement-district-board/; Business Improvement District (GIS feature) [Spokane] https://data-spokane.opendata.arcgis.com/items/6d150d6f1b964a78b933aa4e4aaa038e; Parking and Business Improvement Areas (overview, formation, examples) [Washington State] https://mrsc.org/explore-topics/economic-development/financing/parking-and-business-improvement-areas; Business Improvement Districts (Downtown/Parkway and Uptown) [Richland] https://www.richlandbusiness.com/programs/business-improvement-districts; Parking & Business Improvement Area Board / Municipal Code Ch. 3.62 [Olympia] https://www.olympiawa.gov/government/advisory_committees/parking___business_improvement_area_board.php; Parking & Business Improvement Area (PBIA) [Snohomish] https://www.snohomishwa.gov/767/Parking-Business-Improvement-Area-PBIA; Downtown Yakima Business Improvement District Advisory Board (Municipal Code Ch. 1.33) [Yakima] https://www.codepublishing.com/WA/Yakima/html/Yakima01/Yakima0133.html
next_action: Expand municipal coverage by verifying additional city GIS layers/official lists (Everett Station/Downtown BIAs, East Sprague Spokane, Aberdeen, Port Angeles, others); confirm downloadable boundary data where available; search for any non-governmental statewide association directory or inventory; note any county-level PBIAs.
notes: Enabling statute is Chapter 35.87A RCW (Parking and Business Improvement Areas), authorizing cities/towns/counties to form special assessment districts for economic development, parking, promotion, maintenance, security, etc., via petition (typically ~60% of assessments) or resolution. Locally termed PBIAs/BIAs/BIDs; Tourism Improvement Areas (e.g., Seattle STIA) also use this authority. Authoritative statewide registry or complete inventory not located (MRSC provides examples only). Distinguish carefully from unrelated Local Improvement Districts (LIDs under other RCW chapters, e.g., capital infrastructure assessments) and Public Development Authorities. Multiple active districts confirmed in Seattle (11+ including Metropolitan Improvement District/MID and others), Tacoma (Downtown BIA with GIS), Spokane (Downtown and East Sprague), Richland (2), Olympia, Snohomish, Everett, Yakima, Aberdeen, etc. Municipal GIS available for major cities; many boundaries also in ordinances or PDF maps. No proof of absence elsewhere—coverage incomplete without exhaustive municipal search.
